rulings.Case Title: IN RE: GOLDEN GATE COMPANY, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 8, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER GOLDEN GATE COMPANY, INC.'s MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION. (part 1 of 2) Petitioner Golden Gate Sign Company, Inc.'s petition to compel arbitration is granted. The subcontract between Golden Gate Sign Company, Inc. ("GGS") and Architectural Design & Signs, Inc. ("ADS") states that it will be performed in accordance with the contract between ADS and the general contractor ("WOJV"). (Short Form Standard Subcontract (preface).) The subcontract further provides that "[a]ny claims resolution process incorporated in the prime contract [the ADS/WOJV contract] shall be deemed incorporated in the Agreement and shall apply to any disputes arising hereunder." (Id. § 12.) Section 17 of the ADS/WOJV contract sets forth the claims resolution process. The most reasonable interpretation of that section is that it requires that all disputes or claims between the parties that do not involve the owner of the project, the Transbay Joint Powers Authority, are subject to mandatory arbitration. Section 17.2, entitled "Arbitration Procedures," requires that any party with a claim or dispute that is principally between the parties to the contract and "does not involve the Owner" must give the other party written notice of the claim as a precondition to proceeding with the claim, while section 17.2.3 provides that notice of demand for arbitration shall be filed with the American Arbitration Association within a reasonable time after written notice of claim has been given, and section 17.2.4 governs the arbitration award. Thus, while section 17.2.1 provides that certain claims "shall be decided by litigation," that provision appears to refer only to claims that are not "specifically governed by the foregoing provisions" requiring written notice (i.e., claims that do not involve the owner). Even if the ADS/WOJV contract is ambiguous, the court is obliged to compel arbitration in such a situation. There exists a strong policy in favor of arbitration. (See Madden v Kaiser Foundation Hospitals (1976) 17 Cal.3d 699, 706.) "Doubts as to whether an arbitration clause applies to a particular dispute are to be resolved in favor of sending the parties to arbitration." (Service Employees International Union v. City of Los Angeles (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 136, 143.) "An order to arbitrate the particular grievance should not be denied unless it may be said with positive assurance that the arbitration clause is not susceptible of an interpretation that covers the asserted dispute. Doubts should be resolved in favor of coverage." (Charles J. Rounds Co. v. Joint Council of Teamsters No. 42 (1971) 4 Cal.3d 888, 892 (internal quotations and italics omitted).) In addition, recognized canons of contractual construction militate in favor of arbitration. First, ADS and WOJV were the parties responsible for drafting the arguably ambiguous section 17 and that covenant is construed against ADS. (See Civ. Code § 1654.) Second, "[t]he whole of a contract is to be taken together, so as to give effect to every part, if reasonably practicable, each clause helping to interpret the other." (Civ. Code § 1641.) ADS' proposed construction of section 17 would render large portions of that agreement mere surplusage. The court awards $7,000.00 in fees and costs to GGS for prevailing on the instant petition. (See Benjamin, Weill & Mazer v. Kors (2011) 195 Cal.App.4th 40, 73-80; Acosta v. Kerrigan (2007) 150 Cal.App.4th 1124, 1132; ADS/GGS Short Form Standard Subcontract, § 12.) (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM H BRADLEY VS. DOLMEN PROPERTY GROUP LLC A DELAWARE LIMITED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses
rulings.Rulings: October 8, 2019 Line 1, CROSS DEFENDANT SERVICELINK TITLE CO.'S Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: IT APPEARS FROM THE CALENDAR THAT THIS MATTER MAY HAVE SETTLED AND A DISMISSAL ENTERED. HOWEVER, IT IS UNCLEAR IF THAT INCLUDED THE CROSS-COMPLAINT. ACCORDINGLY, NO OPPOSITION HAVING BEEN FILED TO CROSS-DEFENDANTS SERVICELINK'S MOTION TO COMPEL THE COURT WILL ENTER AN ORDER REQUIRING CROSS-DEFENDANT/CROSSCOMPLAINANT CIT BANK AND CROSS-DEFENDANT/CROSSCOMPLAINANT ONE WEST SERVICE LLC TO SERVE WITHIN TAN (10) DAYS FROM THE DATE OF THIS ORDER FURTHER RESPONSES TO RQUEST FOR ADMISSION, SET ONE, FORM INTERROGATIORIES, SET ONE AND SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES SET TWO AS TO CIT AND SET ONE AS TO ONE WEST SERVICES LLC. THE COURT WILL ASSESS MONETARY DAMAGES AS AGAINST BOTH CIT AND ONE WEST SERVICES LLC COLLECTIVELY IN THE AMOUNT OF $3,955.00 PAYMENT OF WHICH SHOULD BE MADE TO CROSS-DEFENDANT SERVICELINK WITHIN TEN (10) DAYS FROM THE DATE OF THIS ORDER. COUNSEL FOR THE MOVING PARTY SHALL PREPARE AN APPROPRIATE ORDER, SUBMIT A COPY TO RESPONDING PARTIES AND DELIVER A COPY TO THE COURT FOR SINGING AT THE HEARING ON OCTOBER 8, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rennepar85@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (jpt/302)

rulings.Case Title: VINCENT CASTILLO MARENTES ET AL VS. CRUSADER INSURANCE COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For New Trial & Payment Of Court Reporter Fee
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 8, 2019, Line 13, Motion For New Trial & Payment Of Court Reporter Fee. Plaintiff Liudmila Bichegkeuva's motion for new trial is denied. On July 10, 2019, the Court granted defendant Crusader Insurance Company's motion for summary judgment and denied plaintiffs' motion for summary adjudication. On August 7, 2019, judgment was entered in favor of defendant. Defendant served plaintiff by mail with notice of entry of that judgment on August 8, 2019. On August 28, 2019, the Court took off calendar plaintiff's motion for reconsideration. (See Ramon v. Aerospace Corp. (1996) 50 Cal.App.4th 1233, 1236 ["after entry of judgment, a trial court has no further power to rule on a motion for reconsideration"].) Plaintiff did not file the statutorily-mandated notice of intention to move for a new trial within 15 days of the date of mailing notice of entry of judgment, or by August 23, 2019. (Code Civ. Proc. § 659(a).) Nor did plaintiff, within 10 days of filing such a notice, serve and file the supporting brief and accompanying documents. (Id. § 659a.) Instead, on September 9, 2019, plaintiff filed the instant motion for a new trial. The Court lacks jurisdiction to rule on plaintiff's belated motion. (Tri-County Elevator Co. v. Superior Court (1982) 135 Cal.App.3d 271, 277 ["Because compliance with the 15-day requirement of section 659 is jurisdictional [citations], respondent court was without power to entertain the motion [for new trial] and properly denied it."]; Douglas v. Janis (1974) 43 Cal.App.3d 931, 936 [because compliance with the 15-day requirement of section 659 is "jurisdictional," a late notice is "totally ineffectual" and a trial court has "no power to entertain or act on the motion"].) In any event, plaintiffs fail to show that a new trial is warranted, either on the grounds of newly discovered evidence or of an error in law. (Code Civ. Proc. § 657(4),(7).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FRANCISCO MAXEY VS. CALIFORNIA STATE UNIVERSITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT ? Defendants Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 8, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT TIMOTHY BARBER, BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF CALIFORNIA STATE UNIVERSITY's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Defendants Board of Trustees of California State University and T. Barber's motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendants fail to maintain their initial burden of production. Defendants fail to establish as a matter of law that plaintiff was engaged in a "hazardous recreational activity" within the meaning of Gov't Code § 831.7, which immunizes public entities and their employees from liability for personal injury suffered by a person "who participates in a hazardous recreational activity." The evidence suggests that plaintiff was skateboarding as a means of transportation rather than for recreational purposes. (See Gov't Code § 831.7(b)(3) [defining hazardous recreational activity to include mountain bicycling, but excluding "riding a bicycle on paved pathways, roadways, or sidewalks"]; Avila v. Citrus Community College Dist. (2006) 38 Cal.4th 148, 159 [construing "recreational activities" as referring to "voluntary unsupervised play"].) Plaintiff testified that skateboarding was a way of getting from A to B. (Defendants' Ex. B., Maxey Depo., 45:19-20.) The evidence further indicates that plaintiff and his friends chose to skateboard in order to get to the stadium faster. (Defendants' Ex. C., Aliamus Depo., 35:3-6.) The trier of fact needs to resolve this dispute. (See Childs v. County of Santa Barbara (2004) 115 Cal.App.4th 64, 72 [triable issue of fact existed regarding whether child was riding a scooter as a recreational activity and explaining that "[r]iding a scooter as a means of transportation on a public sidewalk is not the same activity as 'scootering' by a number of riders in an organized event."]; cf. Bertsch v. Mammoth Community Water Dist. (2016) 247 Cal.App.4th 1201, 1210-1211 [evidence submitted on summary judgment established that plaintiff was "doing more than riding his skateboard as a means of transportation," but rather was going up a hill "to experience the thrill and excitement of coming back down"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: CAVALRY SPV I, LLC AS ASSIGNEE OF CITIBANK N.A VS. ALLEN E BYERLY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SET ASIDE/VACATE JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 8, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT ALLEN BYERLY's MOTION FOR SET ASIDE/VACATE JUDGMENT. Defendant's motion to vacate the default entered on 4/25/19 and the default judgment entered on 5/2/19 is granted. Defendant demonstrates that he was not properly served with the summons and complaint and that the default/default judgment are void. (See Code of Civ. Proc., § 473(d).) The service of the summons and complaint is quashed. Plaintiff's proof of service for the summons and complaint states that plaintiff personally served a slight 65-year old bald gentleman at 1359 Valencia St., Apt. 1. Defendant's declaration establishes that defendant never resided at that residence and has no physical resemblance to the served sexagenarian. Defendant is a 40-year-old man who weighs 285 lbs. and possesses a full head of hair. That record conclusively rebuts any presumption of proper service. The court observes that plaintiff's brief appears to relate to an entirely different case, as it discusses the service of a woman and argues that "she" provides no proof that she was on an airline flight. (Opp., pg. 2.) Defendant is a man and his declaration has nothing to do with an airline flight. rulings.Case Title: OK SOON KEY VS. COSTCO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reconsideration
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 8, 2019, Line 9, PLAINTIFF OK KEY's Motion For Reconsideration. Plaintiff Ok Soon Key's motion for reconsideration of the Court's August 20, 2019 order on Defendant Costco Wholesale Corporation's demurrer to the first amended complaint is denied. The motion is brought on the false assumption that the Court did not consider Plaintiff's opposition to that demurrer before issuing its ruling. It did. Although the opposition was filed two days late, the Court nevertheless read and considered it before making its ruling. (Had no opposition been filed, the Court would have sustained the demurrer in its entirety without leave to amend.) The motion fails to show any new or different facts, circumstances, or law warranting reconsideration of that order. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1008(a).) That Plaintiff was nominally representing herself at the time of the hearing, albeit with the sub rosa assistance and advice of counsel (Wynn Decl. 1; Meeks Decl. 2), is not grounds for special treatment, much less for reconsideration. (See ViaView, Inc. v. Retzlaff (2016) 1 Cal.App.5th198, 208 ["While a party may choose to act as his or her own attorney, '[s]uch a party is to be treated like any other party and is entitled to the same, but no greater consideration than other litigants and attorneys.'"].) Costco's request for sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KENNETH ALLEN WHITE, II VS. SMULE, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 8, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANT SMULE, INC.'s MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Smule, Inc.'s motion to strike punitive damages is granted. Plaintiff's first amended complaint seeks to state a cause of action for violation of Lab. Code § 970. Lab. Code §972 provides that "any person, or agent or officer thereof who violates any provision of Section 970 is liable to the party aggrieved, in a civil action, for double damages resulting from such misrepresentations." Thus, the Legislature determined that double damages is the appropriate civil penalty for violations of section 970; it did not also authorize an award of punitive damages. Because the Labor Code expressly provides for the award of statutory penalties, "the Legislature's provision of such statutory penalties precludes an award of punitive damages." (In re Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. Wage and Hour Litigation (N.D. Cal. 2007) 505 F.Supp.2d 609, 620 [striking prayer for punitive damages]; see also De Anza Santa Cruz Mobile Estates Homeowners Assn. v. De Anza Santa Cruz Mobile Estates (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 890, 915 ["We have found no persuasive authority for the proposition that a plaintiff is allowed to recover punitive damages based solely on a statutory violation where the statute expressly provides for a penalty that is punitive in nature."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: KENNETH ALLEN WHITE, II VS. SMULE, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 8, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANT SMULE, INC.'s DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Smule, Inc.'s demurrer to the first amended complaint is overruled. A claim for fraud may lie where a misrepresentation induces a plaintiff to enter into a contract, including an employment contract. (See Lazar v. Sup. Ct. (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, 638.) In Lazar, the court upheld recovery, under a claim for fraud, for damages incurred in relocating in reliance on misrepresentations by an employer. (Id. at 649.) However, a claim under Labor Code § 970 is a distinct and separate cause of action. (See id. at 637 [plaintiff "set forth causes of action for: (1) violation of Labor Code section 970 . . . [and] (3) fraud…"].) Defendant's argument that Plaintiff does not allege that Plaintiff reasonably relied on Defendant's representations lacks merit. (See FAC 14 ["Defendant's representations during the pre-hiring process led Plaintiff to reasonably conclude that his job position was long term . . . [which] induced Plaintiff to . . . move his family from Seattle, Washington to the San Francisco Bay Area."].) So does Defendant's argument that Plaintiff does not allege that Defendant knew the representations were false. (See FAC 16 ["The representations by Defendant to Plaintiff of the long-term nature of his job position were false and known to Defendant to be false. Defendant did not intend to abide by its statements..."].) Defendant's argument that Plaintiff does not allege that Mr. Shang had the requisite authority to bind Defendant is overruled. The authority Defendant relies upon requires such pleading when asserting a fraud claim. (Hamilton v. Greenwhich Investors XXVI (2011) 195 Cal.App.4th 1602, 1614 (citing Lazar, 12 Cal.4th at 645).) Defendant does not show that there is such a requirement to plead a claim under Lab. Code § 970, which applies to any "person, or agent or officer thereof . . . ." Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: FRED LARSEN VS. ALISON HAHN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 8, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANT NATALIE TONDELLI-WALSH, TOP DOG PET CARE, LLC(DBA TOP DOG SF),, SERENA GALLEGOS' MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.(part 1 of 2) Defendants Top Dog Pet Care LLC, Natalie Tondelli-Walsh and Serena Gallegos' motion to strike Plaintiff Fred Larsen's prayer for punitive damages in the complaint is granted without leave to amend. A motion to strike is a proper procedural vehicle to strike inadequately pled punitive damages allegations. (See Turman v. Turning Point of Central California, Inc. (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 53, 63-64.) In order to state a prima facie case for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damages statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code § 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate only if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are appropriate only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) Here, the allegations in the complaint that Defendants failed to control a dog, which bit Plaintiff, are insufficient to state a cognizable claim for punitive damages under the foregoing standard. Further, recovery of punitive damages in a dog-bite case appears to the Court to be inconsistent with California law, which imposes strict liability on the owner of an animal that causes personal injuries, but only for compensatory damages, not for punitive damages. (See Priebe v. Nelson (2006) 39 Cal.4th 1112, 1115-1116 ["A common law strict liability cause of action may also be maintained if the owner of a domestic animal that bites or injures another person knew or had reason to know of the animal's vicious propensities. [Citations.] If [defendant] knew or should have known of his dog's vicious propensities and failed to inform [plaintiff] of such facts, he could be found to have exposed [plaintiff] to an unknown risk and thereby be held strictly liable at common law for her (part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FRED LARSEN VS. ALISON HAHN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Natalie Tondelli-Walsh, Top Dog Pet Care, Llc(Dba Top Dog Sf),, Serena Gallegos Motion To Strike Complaint (For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 8, 2019, Line 11, Defendant Natalie Tondelli-Walsh, Top Dog Pet Care, Llc(Dba Top Dog Sf),, Serena Gallegos Motion To Strike Complaint (part 2 of 2) (For Tentative Ruling Only) injuries."]; Civ. Code § 3342 ["dog bite statute" imposing strict liability on the owner of a dog for "the damages suffered by any person who is bitten by the dog while in a public place or lawfully in a private place, including the property of the owner of the dog, regardless of the former viciousness of the dog or the owner's knowledge of such viciousness."]; CACI Nos. 462, 463.) Neither the dog-bite statute nor any reported California case cited by Plaintiff authorizes an award of punitive damages, whether or not the owner was aware of the dog's vicious propensity. "[T]he Legislature has specifically provided in other statutes in other California codes that punitive damages are available, and presumably it was capable of including similar express language in [Civ. Code § 3342] if it so intended." (De Anza Santa Cruz Mobile Estates Homeowners Assn. v. De Anza Santa Cruz Mobile Estates (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 890, 911.) Further, the dog-bite statute applies only to owners of vicious animals; it does not extend to dog-walking businesses or their employees. Finally, the imposition of punitive damages would be inconsistent with California's approach imposing strict liability on owners for such injuries without regard to fault. "'Punitive damages by definition are not intended to compensate the injured party, but rather to punish the tortfeasor whose wrongful action was intentional or malicious, and to deter him and others from similar extreme conduct.'" (Ferguson v. Lieff, Cabraser, Heimann & Bernstein, LLP (2003) 30 Cal.4th 1037, 1046.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANTHONY E HELD PHD VS. FORNEY INDUSTRIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And Consent Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 8, 2019, Line 12, PLAINTIFF ANTHONY HELD's Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And Consent Judgment. The motion by Plaintiff Anthony E. Held, Ph.D., P.E. to approve Proposition 65 settlement and consent judgment is granted. No opposition filed, and the settlement complies with the requirements for Proposition 65 settlements under Health & Safety Code section 25249.7. The Court finds that the warning that is required by the settlement complies with Health & Safety Code sections 25249.5-25249.14; the award of attorney's fees is reasonable under California law; and the penalty amount is reasonable based on the criteria set forth in Health & Safety Code section 25249.7(b)(2). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The court will sign the proposed order and consent judgment included with the moving papers.=(302/EPS)
